comment.body:
https://x.com/RapidResponse47/status/1915468373565276421?t=edYPv-qeXpi8HcPBsrQNpw&s=19

Ukraine wants Crimea back? I mean, okay it's obvious. They talk about it from time to time. But on the other hand they were okayish with not having it for a few years, right?
 They just want to get the most they can out of the deal, don't they. And they think Russia will agree... Ditch the money invested, territory, etc. 

I mean okay they always wanted it, but Crimea wanted to be a little bit more independent, Alma gave some examples how they treated them afterwards. FYI, if you're new and interested in this. 

What would russians think of Putin if he said "okay take it". Alma today touched a very interesting topic related to leaders psychology (among million other things). In their minds, they must be sure they'll be remembered as great leaders, so they can't allow such fuck ups to happen. It must be a victory. Losing territory as aggresor would not be considered a victory, now would it.

comment.body: Amazing Alma. This echoes what I hear from a few China politics commenters that I follow. First it’s typical of communist leaders to weight respect and public perception above all else. And Trump knows this. Second if he even remotely appears to be siding with US, he’s gonna be called a traitor by his PLA generals. Xi is also potentially using this tariff war as an opportunity to flush out naysayers in his party etc.

comment.body: Awesome podcast about global liquidity cycles... helps to contextualize even more Alma's view that Trump/Bessent need a recession... not just for lower equity prices to engage new investment from Russia/ME, but to also take pressure off Bessent who has $9-$10 Trillion of US debt to fund this year. The "term premia", which is located on slide 27, shows the compensation investors require for holding interest rate risk over the term of the bond.
comment.raw_body: Awesome podcast about global liquidity cycles... helps to contextualize even more Alma's view that Trump/Bessent need a recession... not just for lower equity prices to engage new investment from Russia/ME, but to also take pressure off Bessent who has $9-$10 Trillion of US debt to fund this year. The "term premia", which is located on slide 27, shows the compensation investors require for holding interest rate risk over the term of the bond.

comment.body: That is rising while the 10 year yield rate is staying stubbornly high.
comment.raw_body: That is rising while the 10 year yield rate is staying stubbornly high.

comment.raw_body: https://www.macrovoices.com/1413-macrovoices-477-michael-howell-are-we-approaching-a-debt-refinancing-crisis

comment.body: Here's the slide deck: https://www.macrovoices.com/guest-content/list-guest-publications/5787-michealhowell/file
comment.raw_body: Here's the slide deck: https://www.macrovoices.com/guest-content/list-guest-publications/5787-michealhowell/file

comment.body: Also, global "term premia" rates are also rising, making it even more increasingly difficult for Bessent to get lower rates. Compound that with China and others unloading our debt, and we are far from having stability when it comes to liquidity. Also, slide 13 shows the "debt liquidity ratio" where we are coming from a low ratio with high liquidity where bubbles are usually formed. Also, there is a massive "debt maturity wall" where a bunch of very low interest Covid Debt will have to be refinanced... like the $9-$10T Bessent needs to re-up this year.

comment.body: ignore all the labels...the 'upper shelf' came down to 5504 and 5488 -- before we really broke down.  In my experience, the market touches those levels then pulls back.  TBD if later exceeds them significantly.  So I'm short on the after hours action.  We did probe above both levels after the close.

comment.body: Also, there is a 9-10 year "risk cycle" that is covered as well, which we just peaked, on page 20. If you put the podcast on they cover the slides as they go along. Very informative and interesting to see liquidity discussed in this manner. A lot of these cycles are not lining up very well for 25'.

comment.body:
@Alma, I’m studying Jim Simon’s life a bit more and I’m curious on how applicable his main field of study (differential geometry) is to the predictive analysis of financial markets? I understand he wrote his PhD thesis on Riemann manifolds and it seems it’s a field dedicated to the study of defined curved spaces in any dimension. 

The best relation I’m seeing is its relation to Greek and vol surfaces but I’m unsure of how it applies to the analysis of those factors on the options chain

comment.body:
Good day, everyone.
I was reviewing the best option to give you access to the best of Alma’s chats. For now, I’m leaning toward Notion, where I already have all the information ready.
I’m gradually transferring the chats because they included daily, weekly, or geopolitical posts, depending on how Alma shared them in the respective chat. However, we don’t want that information to be leaked, right? So, I’m slowly cleaning it up and will share it with you.
1. For now, I have a subfolder for each day’s chat in Spanish (apologies for the highlighted words—I’ll fix that tomorrow). The idea is for you to also have the chat in your preferred language, so I invite you to let me know—either here or via private message—which language you’d like, and I’ll prepare it.
2. The images where Alma makes notes will also be gradually translated into the corresponding language, and I’ll slowly add the X (Twitter) posts as well.
3. Additionally, I’ll give you access little by little so you can save your files, study them, highlight, take notes, and do whatever you’d like.
I’m open to suggestions for improvement. The idea is for us to work as a team, learn together, unite, and build a pleasant and—above all—well-informed community.
🔗 Links:
https://www.notion.so/Chats-Alma-1e0505449267805a9a7ce95574b5caff?pvs=4
https://paypal.me/SrLex25?country.x=CO&locale.x=es_XC
